1: In the thirty-sixth year of Asa’s reign, Baasha king of Israel comes up against Judah and builds Ramah (Rāmāh) to prevent anyone going out or coming in to Asa king of Judah. The blockade of Ramah (about 5 miles north of Jerusalem) is a choke-point strategy — cutting off Jerusalem from the north. The timing — year 36, after 35 years of peace — marks the moment when the external pressure arrives that will test whether Asa’s covenant faithfulness translates to crisis-trust or political calculation.
2: Asa takes silver and gold from the treasuries of YHWH’s house and the king’s house and sends them to Ben-hadad king of Syria at Damascus to buy a military alliance. The transaction is precisely described: temple treasure + royal treasure = Syrian military service. The Chronicler’s readers would immediately recognize the problem: Asa is using YHWH’s own treasury to fund a political solution that bypasses YHWH’s protection. The temple treasure was given in trust; spending it on a foreign alliance is a form of maʿal (unfaithfulness).
3: Bᵉrît bênî ûḇênêḵā ûḇên-ʾāḇî ûḇên-ʾāḇîḵā hinnēh šālāḥtî lᵉḵā kesep ûzāhāḇ lēḵ hāper ʾet-bᵉrîtᵉḵā ʾet-Baeʿšāʾ melek Yiśrāʾēl wᵉyaʿal mēʿālay — “Let there be a covenant between me and you, as between my father and your father.” Asa uses covenant language (bᵉrît) with a foreign king — the same language used for Israel’s covenant with YHWH. The irony is sharp: the king who entered a covenant with YHWH (15:12) now enters a covenant with Aram using YHWH’s money to fund it.
4: Ben-hadad listens to Asa and sends his commanders against the cities of Israel, striking Ijon, Dan, Abel-maim, and all the store cities of Naphtali. The strategy works militarily: the Syrian assault on the northern cities forces Baasha to withdraw from Ramah. Asa has solved the immediate military problem. But the Chronicler’s theology requires that we read the military success as a spiritual failure: solving the problem by the wrong means is still the wrong means.
5: When Baasha hears it, he stops building Ramah and abandons his work. The tactical withdrawal signals the success of Asa’s Syrian strategy — from a purely military standpoint, the diplomacy worked. But the Chronicler has already established the evaluative framework: what matters is not whether the strategy succeeded but whether Asa sought YHWH. The success of ungodly means does not validate them.
6: Asa takes the stones and timber of Ramah that Baasha had been using and builds with them Geba and Mizpah. The repurposed materials — stones of the enemy’s fortification becoming the materials of Asa’s own — is a small pragmatic victory. But in the Chronicler’s narrative it reads as a footnote: Asa builds with Baasha’s stones while the deeper spiritual deficit goes unaddressed.
7: Hanani the seer comes to Asa and says: bᵉhišŠāʿănᵉḵā ʿal-melek ʾĂrām wᵉlōʾ nišŠāʿantā ʿal-YHWH ʾĕlōheḵā ʿal-kēn niplat ḥêl melek ʾĂrām miyyāḏeḵā — “Because you relied on the king of Syria and did not rely on YHWH your God, the army of the king of Syria has escaped you.” The prophetic verb šāʿan (to rely/lean on) from 14:11 appears now in its negative form: Asa who once relied on YHWH now relies on Ben-hadad. The escape of the Syrian army — which could have been captured had YHWH fought — is what Asa has forfeited by his alliance.
8: Were not the Cushites and the Libyans a huge army with chariots and horsemen exceedingly many? Yet because you relied on YHWH (nišŠāʿantā bYHWH), he gave them into your hand. The prophet reminds Asa of his own history — the Cushite victory of ch14, explicitly cited — to make the contrast concrete: same king, different crisis, different response, different outcome. YHWH’s track record for those who trust him is the basis of the rebuke: you already know what happens when you rely on YHWH; why did you abandon that knowledge?
9: Kî YHWH ʿênāyw mᵉšōṭᵉṭôt bᵉḵol-hāʾāreṣ lᵉhitḥazzēq ʿim-lᵉḇāḇām šālēm ʾēlāyw — “For the eyes of the LORD run to and fro throughout the whole earth, to give strong support to those whose heart is blameless/whole toward him.” This is one of the Chronicler’s most theologically concentrated statements: divine omniscience (ʿênāyw mᵉšōṭᵉṭôt = eyes running to and fro) is not neutral surveillance but active searching for whole-hearted devotion in order to give strength (hitḥazzēq) to those who have it. The verse appears in Zech 4:10 (“seven eyes of YHWH which range through the whole earth”). You have acted foolishly in this — niśkaltā ʿal-zōʾt — the prophetic verdict on pragmatic faithlessness.
10: Asa is angry with the seer and puts him in the stocks (bêt hammahpeket), for he is enraged (zāʿap) at him because of this. Asa becomes the first Judahite king in Chronicles to imprison a prophet — the same movement that Ahab made against Micaiah (18:25-26). The imprisonment of the prophetic word is the political expression of spiritual refusal: if I cannot silence YHWH’s word, I can at least silence YHWH’s messenger. Asa also inflicts cruelties on some of the people at the same time.
11: The acts of Asa, from first to last, are written in the book of the kings of Judah and Israel. The Chronicler’s source citation here is transitional — the good and the bad, the early faithfulness and the late failure, are all on record. The citation of a single source encompassing both “from first to last” is the Chronicler’s way of acknowledging that Asa’s story is mixed: it cannot be summarized as simply success or failure.
12: In the thirty-ninth year of his reign Asa is diseased in his feet (wᵉḥālāh ʾAsāʾ beraglāyw) — severely — yet even in his disease he did not seek YHWH (lōʾ-dāraš ʾet-YHWH), but sought help from physicians (birōpᵉʾîm). The escalating pattern: in year 36, instead of trusting YHWH he trusts Aram; in year 39, instead of seeking YHWH for healing he seeks only physicians. The Chronicler does not condemn physicians as such — the critique is the “only”: Asa sought physicians and did not seek YHWH. The failure of dāraš (seeking) runs from political crisis to physical illness.
13: Asa slept with his fathers, dying in the forty-first year of his reign. The 41-year reign — one of the longest in Chronicles — ends with two years of diseased feet. The death of the great reforming king in his disease, without having returned to YHWH, is the Chronicler’s most sobering statement about covenant momentum: even the best reforms cannot carry a king through decades of accumulated drift. The whole heart (15:17) can be lost piece by piece.
14: They bury him in the tomb he had cut for himself in the city of David. They lay him on a bier filled with spices and various kinds of blended myrrh and make a very great fire in his honor. The royal burial with spices and a great fire of honor was a mark of high esteem — the same honor was given to Asa’s father Asa (actually: see 2 Chr 21:19 for the contrast with Jehoram who received no fire). The elaborate funeral honors indicate that despite his late-reign failures, Asa’s positive legacy was remembered and honored by the people.
